--- SOURCE 1: Microbial Inoculants and Their Impact on Soil Microbial Communities ---
URL: https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3728534/

SUMMARY:
Many reports indicate that application of microbial inoculants can influence, at least temporarily, the resident microbial communities . However, the major concern remains regarding how the impact on taxonomic groups can be related to effects on functional capabilities of the soil microbial communities.

--- SOURCE 3: Soil microbial inoculants for sustainable agriculture: Limitations and ... ---
URL: https://bsssjournals.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1111/sum.12811

SUMMARY:
The burgeoning global market for soil microbial inoculants for use in agriculture is being driven by pressure to increase sustainable crop production.

--- SOURCE 4: Microbial Inoculants for Improving Crop Quality and Human Health ... ---
URL: https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/microbiology/articles/10.3389/fmicb.2018.02213/full

SUMMARY:
Microbial inoculants are beneficiary microorganisms applied to either the soil or the plant in order to improve productivity and crop health. Microbial inoculants are natural-based products being widely used to control pests and improve the quality of the soil and crop, and hence human health. In view of these, this paper aims to summarize the impact of the conventional agricultural inputs (fertilizers, pesticides, and herbicides) on human health and the ameliorating effect of microbial inoculants on these hazards. Due to increasing numbers of people in farming, in the vicinity of farming areas and consumers of contaminated farm products, agricultural chemical inputs have currently become major public health problems (Alori et al., 2017a,b). Citation: Alori ET and Babalola OO (2018) Microbial Inoculants for Improving Crop Quality and Human Health in Africa.

REVIEW article

Front. Microbiol., 19 September 2018

Sec. Plant Pathogen Interactions

Volume 9 - 2018 | [https://doi.org/10.3389/fmicb.2018.02213](https://doi.org/10.3389/fmicb.2018.02213)

This article is part of the Research Topic Improving the Human Health-Promoting Properties of Crop Products by means of Beneficial Microbes[View all 7 articles](https://www.frontiersin.org/research-topics/7081/improving-the-human-health-promoting-properties-of-crop-products-by-means-of-beneficial-microbes/magazine)

Microbial Inoculants for Improving Crop Quality and Human Health in Africa
==========================================================================

[![Image 5: Elizabeth Temitope Alori](https://loop.frontiersin.org/images/profile/291919/74)Elizabeth Temitope Alori](https://loop.frontiersin.org/people/291919)1[![Image 6: Olubukola Oluranti Babalola*](https://loop.frontiersin.org/images/profile/93752/74)Olubukola Oluranti Babalola](https://loop.frontiersin.org/people/93752)2*

*   1 Department of Crop and Soil Science, Landmark University, Omu-Aran, Nigeria
*   2 Food Security and Safety Niche Area, Faculty of Natural and Agricultural Sciences, North-West University, Mahikeng, South Africa

Current agricultural practices depend heavily on chemical inputs (such as fertilizers, pesticides, herbicides, etc.) which, all things being equal cause a deleterious effect on the nutritional value of farm product and health of farm workers and consumers. Excessive and indiscriminate use of these chemicals have resulted in food contamination, weed and disease resistance and negative environmental outcomes which together have a significant impact on human health. Application of these chemical inputs promotes the accumulation of toxic compounds in soils. Chemical compounds are absorbed by most crops from soil. Several synthetic fertilizers contain acid radicals, such as hydrochloride and sulfuric radicals, and hence increase the soil acidity and adversely affect soil and plant health. Highly recalcitrant compounds can also be absorbed by some plants. Continuous consumption of such crops can lead to systematic disorders in humans. Quite a number of pesticides and herbicides have carcinogenicity potential. The increasing awareness of health challenges as a result of consumption of poor quality crops has led to a quest for new and improved technologies of improving both the quantity and quality of crop without jeopardizing human health. A reliable alternative to the use of chemical inputs is microbial inoculants that can act as biofertilizers, bioherbicide, biopesticides, and biocontrol agents. Microorganisms are able to carry out the plant growth promotion, pest and disease and weed control. Microbial inoculants are beneficiary microorganisms applied to either the soil or the plant in order to improve productivity and crop health. Microbial inoculants are natural-based products being widely used to control pests and improve the quality of the soil and crop, and hence human health. Microbial inoculants involve a blend of microorganisms that work with the soil and the soil life to improve soil fertility and health and by extension improve human health. Microbial inoculants have the ability to minimize the negative impact of chemical input and consequently increase the quantity and quality of farm produce. Microbial inoculants are environmental-friendly and deliver plant nutrients to plants in a more sustainable manner. Microbial inoculants can help reduce chemical fertilizer application. Microbial inoculants could include bacteria, fungi and algae. This research summarizes the impact of agricultural chemical inputs on human health. The contribution of microbial inoculants in sustainable maintenance of human health will be expatiated. Advances in microbial inoculants and technology and strategies to explore this natural, user friendly biological resource for sustainable maintenance of plant health will be discussed.

Introduction
------------

The advent of industrial system of agriculture involving the use of chemicals, preservatives, hormones, and antibiotics resulted in increased food growth and production ([Alori and Fawole, 2017b](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/microbiology/articles/10.3389/fmicb.2018.02213/full#B10)). This new technique produces crop and livestock in larger quantities than the sustainable agriculture practiced in the past ([Gilchrist et al., 2007](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/microbiology/articles/10.3389/fmicb.2018.02213/full#B44)). Industrial agriculture is characterized with mono cropping, in which the same crop is grown season after season. Mono cropping reduces the soil’s ability to naturally eliminate pests and replenish nutrients ([Alori and Fawole, 2017a](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/microbiology/articles/10.3389/fmicb.2018.02213/full#B9)). To combat this menace industrial agriculture uses heavy doses of chemical fertilizers and pesticides ([Alori et al., 2017a](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/microbiology/articles/10.3389/fmicb.2018.02213/full#B7)). Similarly, massive quantities of livestock such as cows, chickens, pigs, and turkeys are raised in confined, overcrowded and unsanitary conditions ([Schmidt, 2002](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/microbiology/articles/10.3389/fmicb.2018.02213/full#B96); [Sayre, 2009](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/microbiology/articles/10.3389/fmicb.2018.02213/full#B94)).

Agrochemicals are commonly used in agricultural production to control or prevent diseases, pests and weeds in order to maintain high quality of agricultural products and eliminate or reduce yield losses. With this industrialized system, food is produced at reduced costs and farmers therefore get higher profits from their farm but serious concerns were being raised about health risks resulting from residues in drinking water and food and from occupational exposure ([Alori and Fawole, 2017b](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/microbiology/articles/10.3389/fmicb.2018.02213/full#B10)). [Suyal et al. (2016)](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/microbiology/articles/10.3389/fmicb.2018.02213/full#B104), reiterated that heavy doses of chemical fertilizer, although leading to self-reliance in food production, causes harmful impacts on living organisms and also depreciate the environment. The chemical contaminates the food produced and goes further to alter the normal body functions of the consumer ([Sayre, 2009](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/microbiology/articles/10.3389/fmicb.2018.02213/full#B94)). [Baker et al. (2002)](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/microbiology/articles/10.3389/fmicb.2018.02213/full#B19), reported 75% of pesticide residues in conventionally grown produce. Water supplies are polluted by toxic insecticides, herbicides, and chemical fertilizers used ([Alori et al., 2017b](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/microbiology/articles/10.3389/fmicb.2018.02213/full#B8)).

One of the factors that increase susceptibility of farm workers to the injurious effects of agro-chemicals include language barrier. Most local farmers are illiterates; this hinders their understanding and adoption of safety precautions on labels and training in proper work practices. Lack of background in agriculture by most hired farm workers who use employment in the agricultural sector as an entry-level job may increase health and safety hazards in the agricultural workplace. It is therefore imperative to seek an alternative technology that will boost food production to meet the food requirements of the ever growing world population while minimizing the health hazards they pose to the environment, humans, and farm animals.

Microbial inoculants refer to formulations composed of beneficial microorganisms that play an important role in soil ecosystems for sustainable agriculture. Microbial inoculants are environmentally friendly and are a potential alternative to chemical fertilizers and pesticides ([Babalola and Glick, 2012](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/microbiology/articles/10.3389/fmicb.2018.02213/full#B18)). They are composed of active strains of microorganisms which directly or indirectly stimulate microbial activity and hence improve mobility of nutrients from soil ([Suyal et al., 2016](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/microbiology/articles/10.3389/fmicb.2018.02213/full#B104)). They could be phyto-stimulants, bio-fertilizers or microbial bio-control agents ([Alori et al., 2017b](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/microbiology/articles/10.3389/fmicb.2018.02213/full#B8)). They provide protection against a range of different pathogens and they are effective bio-herbicides ([Babalola, 2010b](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/microbiology/articles/10.3389/fmicb.2018.02213/full#B17)).

In view of these, this paper aims to summarize the impact of the conventional agricultural inputs (fertilizers, pesticides, and herbicides) on human health and the ameliorating effect of microbial inoculants on these hazards. Advances in microbial inoculants and technology and strategies to explore this natural, user friendly biological resource for sustainable maintenance of plant health will be discussed.

Impact of Agricultural Inputs (Fertilizers, Pesticides and Herbicides) on Human Health
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Due to increasing numbers of people in farming, in the vicinity of farming areas and consumers of contaminated farm products, agricultural chemical inputs have currently become major public health problems ([Alori et al., 2017a](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/microbiology/articles/10.3389/fmicb.2018.02213/full#B7),[b](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/microbiology/articles/10.3389/fmicb.2018.02213/full#B8)). Agro-chemical runoff is a major contributor to surface-water contamination ([Wohlfahrt et al., 2010](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/microbiology/articles/10.3389/fmicb.2018.02213/full#B117)). Excess and wrong usage of chemical fertilizers result in soil washing, contamination of ground water, streams, and sea ([Önder et al., 2011](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/microbiology/articles/10.3389/fmicb.2018.02213/full#B79)). Agricultural chemical inputs gain access into human body systems through three major means: (1) oral ingestion, (ii) infiltration through the skin, and (iii) breathing ([Roychowdhury et al., 2014](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/microbiology/articles/10.3389/fmicb.2018.02213/full#B92)). Pesticides have shown long-term resistance in food including vegetables, meat, and fruits, and in the human body ([Battu et al., 2005](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/microbiology/articles/10.3389/fmicb.2018.02213/full#B22)). Quite a number of people are negatively affected by long-term exposure to agro-chemicals, even at low levels ([Kirkhorn and Schenker, 2001](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/microbiology/articles/10.3389/fmicb.2018.02213/full#B61)). The illnesses range from respiratory disorders and musculoskeletal illnesses to dermal and cardiac related diseases. These illnesses are encountered by farm owners, operators, family members, and employees ([Magauzi et al., 2011](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/microbiology/articles/10.3389/fmicb.2018.02213/full#B67)).

Factors that were responsible for the susceptibility of farm owners and workers to agrochemical poisoning include inadequate protective clothing and safety systems, lack of knowledge of the caution code for hazardous agrochemicals ([Magauzi et al., 2011](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/microbiology/articles/10.3389/fmicb.2018.02213/full#B67)). The study in Nepal, India by ([Bhandari, 2014](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/microbiology/articles/10.3389/fmicb.2018.02213/full#B25)), also confirmed that only 2.33% of the farm owners and workers had received training on the hazardous effect of agrochemicals and preventive measures to protect themselves. More also, in developing countries where less than 20% of the world agrochemical production are consumed, agrochemicals have been reported to account for 70% of acute poisoning among working population ([United States Environmental Protection Agency [USEPA], 2016](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/microbiology/articles/10.3389/fmicb.2018.02213/full#B110)). In Nigeria, [Ojo (2016)](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/microbiology/articles/10.3389/fmicb.2018.02213/full#B77), identified the following as factors that aggravate health hazards from pesticide use; the use of the cheaper but deadliest types of pesticides (in terms of persistence and toxicity); poor pesticide education leading to extensive misuse; pesticides residues on locally consumed products, poor legislation and lack of enforcement of available legislation; inadequate information, awareness and knowledge of the inherent dangers of pesticides and inadequacies in medical rec

Review Paper

Deliberate introduction of invisible invaders: A critical appraisal of the impact of microbial inoculants on soil microbial communities
=====================================================================================================================================================

Author links open overlay panel Panji Cahya Mawarda a b, Xavier Le Roux c, Jan Dirk van Elsas a, Joana Falcao Salles a

Show more

Outline

Add to Mendeley

Share

Cite

[https://doi.org/10.1016/j.soilbio.2020.107874](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.soilbio.2020.107874 "Persistent link using digital object identifier")[Get rights and content](https://s100.copyright.com/AppDispatchServlet?publisherName=ELS&contentID=S0038071720301711&orderBeanReset=true)

Under a Creative Commons [license](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0/)

Open access

Highlights
----------

*   •Literature search shows microbial inoculants modify native soil community structure. 
*   •Inoculation-induced changes in soil microbiome can also alter soil functioning. 
*   •The detected inoculation impact depends on measurement time and technical approach. 
*   •Associated mechanisms are competition, antagonism, synergism, changed root exudation. 

Abstract
--------

Non-target effects of deliberately released organisms into a new environment are of great concern due to their potential impact on the biodiversity and functioning of ecosystems. Whereas these studies often focus on [invasive species](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/invasive-species "Learn more about invasive species from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") of macro-organisms, the use of [microbial inoculants](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/microbial-inoculant "Learn more about microbial inoculants from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") is often expected to have specific effects on particular functions but negligible overall effects on resident [microbial communities](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/microbial-community "Learn more about microbial communities from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). Here, we posit that such introductions often impact native microbial communities, which might influence ecosystem processes. Focusing on soil communities, we used a literature search to examine the impact of microbial inoculation (often the release of [beneficial microorganisms](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/beneficial-microorganisms "Learn more about beneficial microorganisms from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") in agricultural systems) on resident microbial communities. Of 108 studies analyzed, 86% showed that inoculants modify [soil microbial communities](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/soil-microbial-community "Learn more about soil microbial communities from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") in the short or long term. In addition, for studies analyzing the consequences of microbial inoculants in the longer term, 80% did not observe the resilience (return to the initial state) of the resident community following inoculation. Through the knowledge gathered from each study, we propose a synthetic and mechanistic framework explaining how inoculants may alter resident microbial communities. We also identify challenges as well as future approaches to shed more light on this unseen reality.

*   [Previous article in issue](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0038071720301796)
*   [Next article in issue](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0038071720301905)

1. Microbial invasions
----------------------

Fortuitous and deliberate introduction of non-native organisms across biogeographic barriers by human activities can perturb and subsequently alter biological diversity over space and time ([Vitousek et al., 1997](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0038071720301711#bib103); [Gaston et al., 2003](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0038071720301711#bib34); [Hulme, 2009](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0038071720301711#bib37)). [Ecologists](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/ecologist "Learn more about Ecologists from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") have shown that the invasion of habitats by exotic macro-organisms poses a significant threat, not only to the extinctions of resident species but also to ecosystem functioning in various environments ([Roy et al., 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0038071720301711#bib81)). Human-mediated invasion (HMI) can decrease native species richness and evenness ([Blackburn et al., 2004](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0038071720301711#bib16)) as well as change the composition ([Shiganova et al., 2001](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0038071720301711#bib92)) and [genetic diversity](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/genetic-divergence "Learn more about genetic diversity from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") of resident communities ([Kreiser et al., 2000](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0038071720301711#bib53); [Kawamura et al., 2006](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0038071720301711#bib48); [Roman and Darling, 2007](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0038071720301711#bib80)). Many studies have focused on the impacts of introducing particular species on resident plant or animal communities ([Pyšek et al., 2012](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0038071720301711#bib73); [Falcão et al., 2017](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0038071720301711#bib30); [Wainwright et al., 2017](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0038071720301711#bib104)). Well-known examples are the effects of introducing predatory species to regulate prey populations on islands, potentially leading to undesired impacts on the native communities ([Kenis et al., 2009](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0038071720301711#bib49); [Bahlai et al., 2015](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0038071720301711#bib10)). Aside from such negative consequences, invasion could also render positive outcomes and be perceived as beneficial. In particular, HMI can increase the abundance of some taxa and promote key ecosystem services ([Simberloff et al., 2013](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0038071720301711#bib94)).

Contrary to large organisms, studies on the impact of microbial invasions are less frequent ([Litchman, 2010](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0038071720301711#bib58)), despite the fact that microbes have been intentionally released into open environments for a long time. Some microorganisms are naturally released to the atmosphere ([Morris et al., 2014](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0038071720301711#bib68)), aquatic ([Amalfitano et al., 2015](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0038071720301711#bib4)), and [terrestrial ecosystems](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/terrestrial-ecosystem "Learn more about terrestrial ecosystems from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") ([Weil et al., 2017](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0038071720301711#bib110)), but for deliberate invasion, it is mostly the case in the environmental/agricultural sector, where introduced microorganisms are used for [soil bioremediation](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/soil-bioremediation "Learn more about soil bioremediation from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), biocontrol, and biofertilization purposes ([Vejan et al., 2016](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0038071720301711#bib101); [Ahmad, 2017](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0038071720301711#bib1)). In addition, microbial releases into soil are emerging as an approach for the conservation or restoration of biodiversity ([Harris, 2009](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0038071720301711#bib36); [Sutherland et al., 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0038071720301711#bib96)). Soil microbial introductions thus aim at regulating or improving ecosystem processes and services such as the promotion of plant yield, [litter decomposition](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/litter-decomposition "Learn more about litter decomposition from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), nutrient cycling and the maintenance of soil fertility ([Ouahmane et al., 2007](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0038071720301711#bib71); [Bounaffaa et al., 2018](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0038071720301711#bib18); [Rodríguez-Caballero et al., 2018](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0038071720301711#bib79); [Tamayo-Vélez and Osorio, 2018](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0038071720301711#bib97)). However, the effects sometimes deviate from the intended purposes. For instance, the introduction of _[Fusarium](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/fusarium "Learn more about Fusarium from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages")_ and _[Rhizoctonia](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/rhizoctonia "Learn more about Rhizoctonia from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages")_ strains to control invasive weeds can lead to a decline in the weed population and suppress the native plant species, through synergistic interactions with root-disrupting insects and other potentially growth-suppressive microbes ([Kremer et al., 2006](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0038071720301711#bib54)). Even though the [soil microbial community](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/soil-microbial-community "Learn more about soil microbial community from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") might have the ability to reorganize and return to the original state (resilience) after the disturbance induced by inoculation, this result highlights the potential ecological and evolutionary impacts of microbial inoculation to soil resident communities, which remain largely unknown. Understanding the effect of microbial inoculation on soil [microbial communities](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/microbial-community "Learn more about microbial communities from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") may be hampered by the overwhelming diversity of the latter and by hurdles in the methods used to characterize this diversity ([Allison and Martiny, 2008](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0038071720301711#bib3); [Le Roux et al., 2011](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0038071720301711#bib56); [Jurburg and Salles, 2015](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0038071720301711#bib45)). Moreover, the assumed ubiquity of microbial species, their rapid growth, and high level of functional redundancy ([Wertz et al., 2007](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0038071720301711#bib111)) may also explain why inoculant-induced changes in the composition of soil microbial communities were either assumed to be insignificant or just overlooked. However, as the use of [microbial inoculants](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/microbial-inoculant "Learn more about microbial inoculants from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") increases with the deployment of sustainable agricultural practices ([Verma et al., 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0038071720301711#bib102)), research needs to better evaluate to what extent such introductions, successful or not, impact the resident microbial communities. Recently, [Trabelsi and Mhamdi (2013)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0038071720301711#bib98) evaluated 15 studies addressing the impact of inoculation on those soil microbial communities they considered mostly significant. Clearly, several of these studies revealed substantial impacts of the inoculants on soil [microbiomes](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/microbiome "Learn more about microbiomes from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). In addition, [Ambrosini et al. (2016)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0038071720301711#bib5) presented an overview of plant-inoculant interactions and their impacts on microbial communities, indicating that these interactions might promote positive effects on soil fertility. Given the relevance of the topic, we present here the results of a systematic literature review on the extent to which soil communities are influenced by microbial releases, whether the soil microbiome is capable of returning to the original state after disturbance (resilient) as well as the mechanisms driving these potential inoculation-induced changes in soil microbial community.

Regarding microbial releases in an agricultural context, the European Regulation Number 1107/2009, Article 24, expects firms or practitioners to demonstrate that there are no ‘unacceptable effects on the environment’, and states that the objective to protect human, animal and environmental health should predominate regarding the objective to increase plant production ([Commission, 2009](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0038071720301711#bib22)). This is open to interpretation, but lack of inoculant persistence in the environment and of important effects of the inoculant on the soil microbiota are often expected, in addition to a significant effect on the targeted [agroecosystem function](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/agroecosystem-function "Learn more about agroecosystem function from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). Actually, microbial releases into soil often result in transient loads of inoculant that quickly fade away with time. For instance, it has been shown that following maize [seed inoculation](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/seed-inoculation "Learn more about seed inoculation from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") with _[Azospirillum lipoferum](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/azospirillum-lipoferum "Learn more about Azospirillum lipoferum from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages")_ CRT1, the inoculant disappeared at the 6-leaves stage ([Florio et al., 2017](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0038071720301711#bib31)). Given this transitory survival, many practitioners and scientists assumed that microbial releases would have negligible effects on the resident soil microbial communities. However, quick disappearance of a bacterial inoculum in soil does not necessarily imply a lack of lasting legacy on the soil resident community. For example, the introduction of non-pathogenic _Escherichia coli_ into soil shifted the niche structure and increased the niche breadth of resident [bacterial communities](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/bacterial-communities "Learn more about bacterial communities from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), leading to changes in the relative abundances of important bacterial genera in soil such as _Bacillus_, _[Pseudomonas](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/pseudomonas "Learn more about Pseudomonas from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages")_, _[Burkholderia](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/burkholderia "Learn more about Burkholderia from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages")_, and _[Bradyrhizobium](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/bradyrhizobium "Learn more about Bradyrhizobium from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages")_ ([Mallon et al., 2018](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0038071720301711#bib63)).

In this review, we posit that the effect of microbial inoculation on soil resident microbial communities is often significant. In the first part, we examine the significance of shifts in resident microbial composition in response to inoculation and their potential to influence soil ecosystem processes. We base our analyses on a systematic literature search and more detailed presentation of selected examples, highlighting that microbial inoculants do not need to be long-lasting in soil to alter resident communities. In the second part, we discuss microbial community resilience and recovery time, i.e. we examine whether the inoculant-induced shifts are transient or persistent. We then present our current understanding of the mechanisms that underly the alteration in resident microbial abundance, structure, and activities. Finally, we describe the current challenges and recommend potential approaches to foster our knowledge in this area.

2. Microbial releases can modify the structure of native soil communities
-------------------------------------------------------------------------

To evaluate whether [microbial inoculants](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/microbial-inoculant "Learn more about microbial inoculants from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") alter soil [microbial community](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/microbial-community "Learn more about microbial community from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") composition, we reviewed studies that addressed the impact of microbial inoculation on soil microbial communities. A search in Web of Science on February 27, 2019 using the keywords (‘‘impact’’, “inocul*” and ‘‘microbial communit*’’) or (“effect”, “inocul*” and ‘‘microbial communit*’’) in their titles, abstract, or subject words generated 855 references. Screening process on their abstracts and titles reduced the 855 hits to 125 relevant articles ([Fig. 1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0038071720301711#fig1)a). Studies that were not conducted in soil, did not employ microbial inoculation, and whose impact did not refer to native soil microbial communities, were excluded. The full text of each of these 125 articles was assessed; from these, 17 studies that relied only on [plate counts](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/plate-count "Learn more about plate counts from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") were excluded due to methodological issues associated with cultivation constraints. From the remaining 108 studies, 86 used bacterial inoculants, 22 inoculated fungi, while only 2 used the combination of both. All included proper control samples and when inoculation implied soil disturbance (e.g. sowing with seeds coated with an inoculant in [Florio et al., 2017](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0038071720301711#bib31)), we verified that the control included the same disturbance (e.g. sowing with non-inoculated seeds). We further grouped the studies into three categories according to the method used to measure the impact on resident soil microbial communities: 26 studies used high throughput sequencing (HTS) ([Figs. 1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0038071720301711#fig1)b), 78 used profiling methods including molecular, fatty acid, and physiological profiling ([Fig. 1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0038071720301711#fig1)c), and 4 used [quantitative PCR](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/real-time-polymerase-chain-reaction "Learn more about quantitative PCR from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") targeting particular taxonomic or functional groups ([Fig. 1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0038071720301711#fig1)d). Here, we decided to group studies that used profiling methods along with HTS in an HTS method cluster while studies that used sanger sequencing of [amplicon](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/amplicon "Learn more about amplicon from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") clone libraries derived from specific [DGGE](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/denaturing-gradient-gel-electrophoresis "Learn more about DGGE from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") bands were included in a profiling method cluster.

![Image 8: Fig. 1](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S0038071720301711-gr1.jpg)
1.   [Download: Download high-res image (882KB)](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S0038071720301711-gr1_lrg.jpg "Download high-res image (882KB)")
2.   [Download: Download full-size image](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S0038071720301711-gr1.jpg "Download full-size image")

Fig. 1. Flow diagrams describing the process of article selection and screening of selected articles based on the presence/absence of impact, multiple measurement of impact, and evaluation of post-inoculation resilience. [Fig. 1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0038071720301711#fig1)a describes the selection process of studies which consist of abstract screening and full text assessment, providing the reasons of possible exclusion of a given study. [Fig. 1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0038071720301711#fig1)b describes the identification of studies employing high-throughput sequencing (HTS). Studies that used profiling methods along with HTS were included in HTS method. [Fig. 1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0038071720301711#fig1)c describes the identification of studies employing profiling methods, including molecular (i.e. DGGE, TRFLP, ARISA), fatty acid (i.e. FAME, PLFA) and physiological (CLPP) profiling methods. Studies that used Sanger sequencing of [amplicon](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/amplicon "Learn more about amplicon from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") clone libraries derived from specific DGGE bands were included in profiling method. [Fig. 1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0038071720301711#fig1)d describes the identification of studies employing qPCR methods.

The complete list of HTS method-based studies with all related information and parameters is displayed in [Table 1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0038071720301711#tbl1). The lists of the 78 studies using profiling methods and of the 4 qPCR-based studies can be found in the supplementary document ([Tables S1 and S2](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0038071720301711#appsec1)). The result showed that, in over 96% of the HTS studies, microbial release led to changes in microbial community composition. For studies using profiling methods, 82% showed an impact following inoculation whereas 18% did not report any significant effect. Those corresponded to 14 studies based on molecular profiling such as DGGE and [T]RFLP. Regarding studies using qPCR targeting taxonomic or functional groups, all of them reported a significant impact ([Fig. 2](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0038071720301711#fig2)). In general, 30% of the studies using DGGE, TGGE, or [T]RFLP did not report any significant effect of inoculation whereas the other methods did, highlighting that the outcome might be associated with the method used to characterize inoculant effect of the soil resident community. Furthermore, studies that used HTS in combination with other methods (11%) indicate that impact was observed for all methods tested. Keeping in mind these methodological limitations, the data presented in the 108 studies allow us to draw a few generalizations.

Table 1. Studies evaluating the impact of microbial inoculation on soil microbial communities based on a High-Throughput Sequencing method.

enriched_points.expanded_content: Microbial inoculants enhance soil structure, fertility, and biodiversity, thereby promoting sustainable agricultural practices and improving overall soil health.
Utilizing microbial inoculants reduces chemical pesticide usage, minimizing toxic residues in food and improving the nutritional quality of crops, which directly benefits human health.
Numerous studies demonstrate the successful application of microbial inoculants in various crops, enhancing yield and resilience while showcasing their potential in sustainable farming.
Future studies should focus on long-term effects of microbial inoculants on soil communities, exploring innovative formulations and their role in climate-resilient agriculture.
